summary:
The Assassination of Julius Caesar (March 15, 44 BC) — the Ides of March — was one of antiquity's most dramatic political acts: the stabbing of Rome's dictator by a group of approximately sixty senators, led by Marcus Junius Brutus and Gaius Cassius Longinus, who believed they were preserving the Republic by eliminating a tyrant. The assassination achieved the opposite of its intended effect, triggering civil wars that finally destroyed the Republic they sought to save.

Caesar had accumulated unprecedented powers: perpetual dictator, imperator, and honors approaching divinity. Many senators genuinely feared he intended to make himself king — the one title Rome's republican tradition most deeply despised — and his provocative behavior, including sitting on a golden throne during ceremonies and reportedly declining the crown Antony offered him (while appearing to want it), fed these fears. The conspirators, who called themselves Liberatores, convinced themselves that killing Caesar would allow the Republic to function again as before.

On the Ides of March, the conspirators attacked Caesar in the Senate meeting room attached to Pompey's Theater. He was stabbed twenty-three times; according to the physician Antistius, only one wound was fatal. Ancient tradition holds that when he saw Brutus among the attackers, he said in Greek 'kai su, teknon' ('you too, child' — the origin of Shakespeare's 'Et tu, Brute?'). Caesar's body was carried home by three slaves.

The conspirators had no plan for what came next. They expected grateful Romans to restore the Republic; instead, Mark Antony's oration at Caesar's public funeral incited a mob that drove the Liberatores from Rome. Caesar's will named his great-nephew Octavian as heir and left money to every Roman citizen — generating enormous popular sympathy. The ensuing power struggle between Antony, Octavian, and Caesar's killers produced the [[event:1080|Second Triumvirate]], the [[event:1081|Battle of Philippi]], and ultimately the [[event:1083|Augustus - First Roman Emperor]]. The assassination proved that individuals could be killed but the forces reshaping Rome could not.
